title: Stunting vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: [Stunting](#dod:stunting) measures the share of children younger than 5 years that are too short for their age. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Stunting vs. GDP per capita

Stunting measures the share of children younger than 5 years that are too short for their age. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot displays the relationship between the share of children under five who are stunted and GDP per capita for various countries in 2024. The vertical axis represents the percentage of stunted children, ranging from 0% to over 50%, while the horizontal axis plots GDP per capita in international dollars at 2021 prices on a logarithmic scale from roughly $1,000 to $100,000. Points are color-coded by continent, with Africa in purple, Asia in teal, South America in brown, North America in orange, Europe in blue, and Oceania in light blue. A general downward trend is visible, where countries with lower GDP per capita tend to have higher stunting rates. Burundi sits at the highest point on the chart, with a stunting share exceeding 50% and a GDP per capita below $2,000. Niger, the Democratic Republic of Congo, and East Timor also appear in the upper left quadrant, with stunting rates between 40% and 50% and low GDP values. In contrast, countries like Brunei, Estonia, and Belarus are positioned in the bottom right, showing stunting rates near or below 5% and GDP per capita figures exceeding $20,000. A cluster of African nations, including Somalia, Liberia, and Malawi, occupies the mid-left area with stunting rates around 20% to 30% and GDPs under $5,000. Asian countries such as India, Pakistan, and Bangladesh form a group in the middle of the chart with stunting rates between 30% and 35%. European and North American countries generally cluster in the lower right section, indicating lower stunting shares and higher economic output per person.
